title: About
text:
Hi, I’m Tiara — a data scientist / analyst working across Python and R.
This site collects write-ups of projects I’ve built or analyses I’ve run. Each post is a reproducible document: the code blocks you see are the actual code that produced the figures and tables alongside them.

title: About
text:
Tools I reach for

Python: pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib, polars
R: tidyverse, ggplot2, tidymodels
Storage & pipelines: Snowflake, dbt, dlt, Airflow
Reporting: Quarto, Rmarkdown, Jupyter

title: Hello, Python
text: This post is a .qmd file with Python code chunks. Quarto runs the code at render time and inlines the output (numbers, tables, plots) into the page. To start a new post, copy this folder and edit index.qmd.

title: Hello, Python
text:
A tiny dataset

import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "month": pd.date_range("2026-01-01", periods=6, freq="MS"),
    "revenue": [120, 138, 155, 162, 180, 205],
    "channel": ["organic"] * 6,
})
df

title: Hello, Python
text:
A quick plot

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3.5))
ax.plot(df["month"], df["revenue"], marker="o")
ax.set_title("Monthly revenue, organic channel")
ax.set_ylabel("Revenue ($k)")
ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Hello, Python
text:
Notes

Use jupyter: python3 in the YAML header to enable Python execution.
Heavy outputs (model training, big queries) get cached on first render via freeze: auto in _quarto.yml — only the chunks you change get re-executed.

title: Hello, Quarto
text:
Markdown is an easy to read and write text format:

It’s plain text so works well with version control
It can be rendered into HTML, PDF, and more
Learn more at: https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/

title: Hello, Quarto
text:
Equation
Use LaTeX to write equations:
\[
\chi' = \sum_{i=1}^n k_i s_i^2
\]

title: Hello, R
text: This post is an .Rmd file rendered by Quarto. Same chunk syntax you know from Rmarkdown — Quarto picks up the knitr engine automatically when it sees R chunks.

title: Hello, R
text:
A tiny dataset

library(tibble)
library(dplyr)

sales <- tibble(
  month   = seq(as.Date("2026-01-01"), by = "month", length.out = 6),
  revenue = c(120, 138, 155, 162, 180, 205),
  channel = "organic"
)
sales

# A tibble: 6 × 3
  month      revenue channel
  <date>       <dbl> <chr>  
1 2026-01-01     120 organic
2 2026-02-01     138 organic
3 2026-03-01     155 organic
4 2026-04-01     162 organic
5 2026-05-01     180 organic
6 2026-06-01     205 organic

title: Hello, R
text:
A quick plot

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(sales, aes(month, revenue)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Monthly revenue, organic channel",
       x = NULL, y = "Revenue ($k)") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hello, R
text:
Notes

Quarto renders .Rmd files the same way it renders .qmd files with R chunks. If you prefer the newer Quarto syntax, just rename the file to index.qmd.
Use chunk options as YAML-style #| comments (Quarto convention), or the classic inline {r, fig.width=6} form — both work.
